findings.rationale: A keyboard trap was detected on input#c24-travel-destination-element. The element is visited consecutively (steps 23, 24, 25) indicating focus cannot move away. Both Escape and Shift+Tab failed to move focus from the stuck element, confirming no standard keyboard escape mechanism works. With 217 total focusable elements but only 23 unique selectors visited across 25 tabs, and focus cycling on a single input, keyboard users are trapped and cannot access the remaining page content.

This is a 1×1 pixel tracking/analytics image (siteview-static.gif) with alt="" and no aria-label. The screenshot confirms a real (though invisible) image is rendered. The element is correctly marked as decorative via alt="", which is the standard pattern for tracking pixels that convey no information to users. No accessible name is required.

findings.rationale: The element is marked as decorative with alt="" and is not inside a functional link or button. An empty alt attribute is the correct markup for decorative images. The surrounding context (vacation deals promotional text) suggests this is likely a decorative image supporting marketing content rather than conveying independent information.

findings.selector: body > div:nth-of-type(1) > div:nth-of-type(6) > div:nth-of-type(3) > div > div:nth-of-type(2) > div:nth-of-type(4) > div > div:nth-of-type(2) > div:nth-of-type(2) > img
findings.rationale: The image has an empty alt attribute (alt="") and no other accessible name source (no aria-label, aria-labelledby, or parent link/button context). Although marked as decorative via the empty alt, the surrounding context "Antalya & Belek" suggests this image likely conveys informative content (possibly a destination or location image) rather than being purely ornamental. An image that appears to serve an informative purpose cannot be marked decorative with an empty alt; it requires a meaningful text alternative.

findings.selector: body > div:nth-of-type(1) > div:nth-of-type(6) > div:nth-of-type(3) > div > div:nth-of-type(2) > div:nth-of-type(4) > div > div:nth-of-type(2) > div:nth-of-type(3) > img
findings.rationale: The image has an empty alt attribute (alt="") and no other accessible name source (no aria-label, aria-labelledby, or parent link/button context). Although marked as decorative via the empty alt, the surrounding context "Side & Alanya" suggests this is likely an informative image (possibly a location photo or icon) rather than purely ornamental. Without a screenshot, the empty accessible name cannot be verified as appropriate, and the decorative marking appears inconsistent with the contextual text implying informative content.

Without a screenshot, the actual content cannot be verified, but the empty accessible name combined with contextual text indicating informative intent fails SC 1.1.1.

findings.rationale: The image is informative (not decorative, not functional) and depicts a logo with the text 'ITS' and decorative wave elements. The alt text 'Its' is inadequate because it is a generic, incomplete word fragment that does not clearly describe what the image shows. A proper alternative should identify this as a logo or brand mark (e.g., 'ITS logo' or 'ITS company logo'), not a single word that appears to be a typo or truncation.

This is a 1×1 pixel tracking/analytics image (a common web beacon pattern). The screenshot confirms a real, albeit invisible, image is rendered. The element has alt="" and is marked as decorative (marked as decorative: true), which is the correct and standard approach for non-informative tracking pixels. No accessible name is required for genuinely decorative content.

This is a 1×1 pixel tracking/analytics image (a common web analytics pattern). The screenshot confirms a real, albeit invisible, image is rendered. It has alt="" and is not marked as decorative via role/aria-hidden, but the empty alt text combined with its 1×1 size, absolute positioning, and tracking URL pattern indicates it is genuinely decorative/non-informative content. The empty alt text is the correct and appropriate treatment for such tracking pixels.

This is a 1×1 pixel tracking/analytics image (a common web analytics pattern). The screenshot confirms a real, albeit invisible, image is rendered. It is correctly marked as decorative with alt="" and is not inside a functional link/button. The empty alt text is the appropriate treatment for a non-informative tracking pixel that serves no user-facing purpose.

This is a 1×1 pixel tracking/analytics image (a common web analytics pattern). The screenshot confirms a real, albeit invisible, image is rendered. It is marked decorative with alt="" and is not inside a functional link/button. Tracking pixels serve no informative purpose to users and are correctly marked as decorative.

findings.rationale: The link is located on a vacation/travel booking page (urlaub.check24.at), but points to a loan calculator (Kreditrechner) on a different product section. The accessible name 'Kreditrechner' is clear in isolation, but there is no surrounding context explaining why a loan calculator link appears on a travel booking page, making the link's purpose confusing and potentially erroneous within this context.
